Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.

You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

Let me reconsider - maybe the goal is to make the bottom-right match the bottom-left exactly (including center), meaning I need to undo my outer grid changes.
- Plan: Undo my changes to make bottom-right match bottom-left exactly: change (0,2) from R back to b, and (2,2) from b back to R.

end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now the bottom-right grid matches the bottom-left exactly: R b b / R . R / b b R. But the center cells are still different. The level still isn't complete.
Let me think about this differently. Maybe the goal isn't about making the bottom-right match the bottom-left or applying a symmetry transformation. Let me look at ALL four grids and think about the relationship.
Top-left: b R b / R . b / b R R
Top-right: b R b / R . R / b R b
Bottom-left: R b b / R . R / b b R
Bottom-right: R b b / R . R / b b R (now matching bottom-left)
Wait, what if the transformation is column-based instead of row-based? Let me look at the columns:
Top-left columns:
Col 0: [b, R, b]
Col 1: [R, ., R]
Col 2: [b, b, R]
Top-right columns:
Col 0: [b, R, b]
Col 1: [R, ., R]
Col 2: [b, R, b]
Changes: col 2 changed from [b, b, R] to [b, R, b]
This is making col 2 match col 0! The right column becomes a copy of the left column.
Let me verify: for bottom-left:
Col 0: [R, R, b]
Col 1: [b, ., b]
Col 2: [b, R, R]
If the rule is "copy col 0 to col 2":
Col 2 becomes [R, R, b]
So the target bottom-right would be:
R b R
R . R
b b b
That's the symmetric version I had before! But that didn't complete the level either.
Hmm, let me try another interpretation. What if the rule is "make each column a palindrome"?
Top-left col 0: [b, R, b] -> already palindrome
Top-left col 1: [R, ., R] -> already palindrome
Top-left col 2: [b, b, R] -> make palindrome: [b, b, b] or [R, b, R] or [b, R, b]
Top-right col 2: [b, R, b] -> palindrome (top element copied to bottom)
So the rule might be "copy the top element of each column to the bottom element".
Top-left col 2: [b, b, R] -> copy b to bottom: [b, b, b]. But top-right col 2 is [b, R, b], not [b, b, b].
That doesn't work either.
Let me try "make each column a palindrome by copying top to bottom":
Top-left col 2: [b, b, R] -> [b, b, b]. But top-right col 2 is [b, R, b].
Nope.
What about "swap the top and bottom of the right column"?
Top-left col 2: [b, b, R] -> swap: [R, b, b]. But top-right col 2 is [b, R, b].
Nope.
What about "reverse the right column"?
Top-left col 2: [b, b, R] -> reverse: [R, b, b]. But top-right col 2 is [b, R, b].
Nope.
Let me try "make each column a palindrome by copying the first element to the last":
Col 2: [b, b, R] -> [b, b, b]. But top-right col 2 is [b, R, b].
Nope.
Hmm, let me try "mirror the left column to the right column":
Col 0: [b, R, b] -> copy to col 2: [b, R, b]. Top-right col 2 IS [b, R, b]. Yes!
So the rule is "copy column 0 to column 2".
For bottom-left:
Col 0: [R, R, b] -> copy to col 2: [R, R, b]
Target bottom-right:
R b R
R . R
b b b
That's the symmetric version again!
OK so both the row-based and column-based interpretations give the same result for the bottom-right outer grid. And I've tried both the symmetric version and the copy-of-bottom-left version. Neither completed the level.
Maybe the issue is that I need to also change the center cell, but through a different mechanism. Or maybe I need to click on the top grids to trigger something.
Let me try clicking on cells in the top grids to see if they're interactive.
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider the problem. What if the bottom-right grid is NOT the target? What if the goal is to modify the TOP grids somehow?
Or what if the answer involves a completely different set of changes? Let me look at the initial state again.
Initial bottom-right (from history): R b b / R . R / b b R
This matches the bottom-left exactly. So the initial state already has the bottom-right matching the bottom-left. That means the puzzle isn't about making them match.
The transformation must be about changing the bottom-right to something different from the bottom-left.
Let me go back to the symmetric version: R b R / R . R / b b b. I had this before and the level wasn't complete. But maybe I need to ALSO change the center cell to be symmetric.
Wait, maybe the center cell IS clickable but I need to click on specific positions. Let me try clicking on the exact center of each sub-cell in the center cell.
Actually, let me try clicking on the white and gray areas of the center cell more carefully. The center cell has sub-cells at specific positions. Let me try clicking on the center of each sub-cell.
For the bottom-right center:
- Cell (0,0): rows 44-45, cols 44-45 -> click at (44, 44)
- Cell (0,1): rows 44-45, cols 46-47 -> click at (44, 46)
- Cell (0,2): rows 44-45, cols 48-49 -> click at (44, 48)
- Cell (1,0): rows 46-47, cols 44-45 -> click at (46, 44)
- Cell (1,1): rows 46-47, cols 46-47 -> click at (46, 46) (red center)
- Cell (1,2): rows 46-47, cols 48-49 -> click at (46, 48)
- Cell (2,0): rows 48-49, cols 44-45 -> click at (48, 44)
- Cell (2,1): rows 48-49, cols 46-47 -> click at (48, 46)
- Cell (2,2): rows 48-49, cols 48-49 -> click at (48, 48)
I already tried (45, 46) which is in cell (0,1). Let me try (44, 48) which is cell (0,2) - the gray area.
Actually, I already tried clicking on the center cell and it didn't change. Let me try a different approach.
What if I need to click on the top grids? Let me try clicking on a cell in the top-left grid.

sections.content:
World model: Bottom-right now matches bottom-left (R b b / R . R / b b R), but level still not complete. The symmetric version (R b R / R . R / b b b) also didn't complete the level. sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me first check what the current state looks like
lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')

print("Bottom-right outer grid:")
for r in [38, 46, 54]:
    row_str = lines[r]
    # Extract the 3 cells: cols 36-41, 44-49, 52-57
    c0 = row_str[36:42]
    c1 = row_str[44:50]
    c2 = row_str[52:58]
    print(f"  Row {r}: {c0} {c1} {c2}")

# Try clicking on a cell in the top-left grid to see if it's interactive
# Top-left grid cells: rows 2-7, 10-15, 18-23; cols 2-7, 10-15, 18-23
# Let me try clicking on (1,2) of top-left: row 12, col 20
predict(expect_change=True, note='Testing if top-left grid cells are clickable')
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 12, 'col': 20}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
